data.title: U.S. stock futures turn lower after hours as Trump outlines reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
U.S. stock index futures turned lower in Wednesday's after-hours trading, as President Donald Trump outlined import tariffs on products coming worldwide into the U.S.

The minimum baseline tariff will be 10%. Trump spoke at the White House's Rose Garden after trading in the day session closed. As pre-announced, the U.S. will impose a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles as of midnight.

Benchmark S&P 500 futures (SPX) fell -1.7%, and Nasdaq-100 futures (US100:IND) were -2.3%. Dow futures (INDU) shed -0.4%. The futures indexes had each popped up more than 1% as Trump's press event got underway. 

Among the tariff plans, the U.S. will place a 34% levy rate on goods from China, a 24% rate for Japanese goods, a 20% rate on European Union products, and a 10% levy on U.K. and Brazilian products. 

Stocks closed the regular-session higher after spending most of the day in positive territory.

In the bond market, the the 10-year yield (US10Y) fell 5 basis points to 4.12%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) lost 5 basis points to 3.84%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: India, China reciprocal tariffs will impact pharma, biotech industries
data.description:
India, China, and the European Union, which produce the vast majority of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) used in drugs and also manufactured finished products, will face reciprocal tariffs from the United States, effective midnight April 3.

Speaking at an event in the White House Rose Garden Wednesday, President Donald Trump said that pharmaceutical tariffs were needed to ensure adequate supply in case of wars or other potential supply issues.

The country tariffs are as follows: India (26%); China (34%); and European Union (20%).

Trump noted that the reciprocal tariffs on the trio are much less than the tariffs they impose on American goods: India (52%); China (67%); and European Union (39%).

According to the U.S. Pharmacopeia, as of 2021, India manufactured nearly half -- 48% -- of the APIs made into drugs consumed in the U.S. Europe made 22%, and China, 13%. Just 10% of APIs in 2021 were manufactured in the U.S.

A 2022 evaluation of the top 100 Medicare Part D drugs based on spend from PharmacyChecker Research found that 32 were finished in the U.S. and another 67 were finished in countries including European Union countries, Canada, Japan, Singapore, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom.

Most APIs are used in the manufacture of generic drugs, which account for 9 out of 10 prescriptions filled in the U.S.

That means generic drugmakers, such as Viatris (NASDAQ:VTRS) and Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (NYSE:TEVA), could see significant impact.

In his comments, Trump noted that several large drugmakers, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) have recently made announcements of making manufacturing investments in the U.S.

data.title: Fluor to provide EPCM services for multibillion-dollar Indiana pharma project
data.description:
Fluor (NYSE:FLR) said post-market Wednesday that it received a letter of intent from "one of the world's leading pharmaceutical makers" to provide engineering, procurement and construction management services for its multibillion-dollar project in Lebanon, Indiana; financial terms were not disclosed.

Fluor (NYSE:FLR) said the facility, which is in addition to its first multibillion-dollar project that was awarded to the company in 2023, will manufacture peptide-based drugs for Type 2 diabetes and weight control and represent the largest investment in an active pharmaceutical ingredient facility in U.S. history.

"This monumental project stands as one of the largest life science endeavors ever undertaken in a single initiative and demonstrates Fluor's EPCM leadership position in the sector," said Richard Meserole, head of the company's Advanced Technologies & Life Sciences business.

data.title: Logistic Properties of the Americas reports FY results
data.description:
• Logistic Properties of the Americas press release (NYSE:LPA): FY Revenue of $43.86M (+11.2% Y/Y).
  • 2024 Net Operating Income (NOI) increased 7.1% to $36.6 million, while Same-Property Cash NOI increased 5.0% to $33.9 million, primarily due to rent growth and the expiration of rent abatements.
  • On December 31, 2024, the occupancy rate of LPA’s operating portfolio was 98.3%, as compared to 100% at year-end 2023.

data.title: Footwear fallout: Nike, Dick's, and Skechers slide as dueling tariff war escalates
data.description:
The footwear sector saw some selling pressure in the postmarket session after the Trump Administration announced some of the new tariffs that will go into effect this week.

The U.S. will place a 34% tariff rate on goods from China, 46% rate on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 25% on South Korea, 31% on Switzerland, 32% on Taiwan, 36% on Thailand, 20% on the European Union, 24% on Malaysia, 10% on the U.K. and Brazil, and 24% for Japan.

Notable decliners included Nike (NYSE:NKE) -6.6%, Skechers (SKX) -6.1%, Deckers Outdoor (DECK) -3.8%, On Holding (ONON) -10.6%, Steve Madden (SHOO) -2.5%, and Crocs (CROX) -3.9%.

Retailers Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS) -4.4% Sportsman's Warehouse (SPWH) -4.4%, and Foot Locker (FL) -3.1% were also lower during the late session.

data.title: Kenon reports FY results
data.description:
• Kenon press release (NYSE:KEN): FY Revenue of $751M (+8.5% Y/Y).
  • OPC's net profit in 2024 was $53 million, as compared to a net profit of $47 million in 2023. OPC's 2024 net profit included its share in profit of CPV of $45 million as compared to $66 million in 2023.- OPC's Adjusted EBITDA including proportionate share in Adjusted EBITDA of associated companies[1] in 2024 was $332 million as compared to $304 million in 2023.

data.title: White House moves closer to OKing TikTok U.S. sale to American investors
data.description:
The White House is reportedly close to endorsing a deal for investors to buy TikTok U.S. as it aims to get more control of the American portion of the business from its Chinese owners.

According to a report from the Financial Times on Wednesday, citing sources, a group of new outside investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Blackstone (NYSE:BX), and other large private capital firms would own about half of TikTok U.S.

The U.S. unit, which would be spun off from its parent ByteDance, would include large existing investors, namely General Atlantic, Susquehanna, KKR (NYSE:KKR), and Coatue, who would also take stakes constituting about 30% of the business, the report said. ByteDance (BDNCE) would own just below 20% of the business, under the terms of the deal.

The Information had reported that TikTok America would be roughly 50% owned by new U.S. investors.

The plans are still in the preliminary stages and could change, sources told FT, as the sale-or-ban law deadline looms for the short-form content app, which is used by nearly half of the country's population.

Trump administration officials were set to meet on Wednesday to discuss the negotiations, and if the president gave his blessing, the deal could be announced imminently, people told FT.

One person told FT that the investor group would have three to four months to complete the spin-off process. Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), which already manages TikTok's cloud data in the U.S., would get the same as part of the deal, people said.

However, which entity would get control over TikTok’s algorithm remains unknown, several people told FT.

The report said that as an option, ByteDance would continue to develop and operate the algorithm, while the new U.S. group, through a licensing deal, would access it. But analysts say the algorithm needs to be fully operated by the U.S. in order to meet the requirements of the law.

Earlier in the day, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) came in with a last-minute bid for TikTok’s U.S. business, as per a New York Times report.

data.title: GE Vernova to supply turbines for planned huge natural gas power plant in Pennsylvania
data.description:
GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) closed +4.8% on Wednesday following news it will supply seven wind turbines producing nearly 4.5 GW of power in turning Homer City Generating Station in Pennsylvania from a coal-fired power plant into a natural gas-powered data center campus, with production expected to begin in 2027.

Once complete, the Homer City plant would be the largest natural gas power plant in the U.S., surpassing NextEra Energy's West County Energy Center in Florida, which has a capacity of nearly 3.8 GW, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

In a release from Homer City Redevelopment, which is in charge of the project, it said construction is expected to begin this year, and GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) will supply the turbines by 2026.

The demolition of the coal plant began last month as Homer City Redevelopment imploded the cooling towers and three out of four of the facility's stacks, with the rest of the plant expected to come down over the next couple of weeks.

The project is expected to cost more than $10B, without including the costs necessary for building the AI data centers expected to be built on the same campus.

Separately, GE Vernova (GEV) won a new Positive rating at Susquehanna, which said the company is well positioned to benefit from global growth in power demand.

data.title: Trump announces reciprocal tariffs, charging half of other nation's tariffs on US (updated)
data.description:
President Donald Trump announced that he's enacting reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday, which he has dubbed "Liberation Day."

As he had pre-announced, the U.S. will impose a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles as of midnight tonight.

4:26 PM ET: The U.S. will calculate all tariffs and non-monetary barriers placed by other nations against the U.S. and charge those "approximately half" of what they charge us, he said. "How can anybody be upset?" Trump said, reasoning that the U.S. isn't charge that full amount.

"They do it to us, we do it to them," he said. 

"Our taxpayers and country have been ripped off for 50 years. That’s not going to happen anymore," he said.

He said we'll use "trillions and trillions of dollars to pay down national debt... Jobs and factories will come roaring back."

Trump contended that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices.

Updated at 4:24 PM ET: In a period before 1913, the U.S. relied heavily on tariffs and was much richer, Trump claimed. He seems to blame the Great Depression on stopping the previous tariff policy. (However, the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, enacted in 1930, is widely blamed for putting the U.S. deeper into the Depression.)

Developing... check back for updates.
